data.title: Chicago rents surge as housing shortage deepens, construction stalls
data.description:
Chicago’s reputation as a relatively affordable big city is eroding as rents climb sharply, driven by a persistent shortage of new housing and a slowdown in construction, Bloomberg News reported Monday.

Average monthly rent reached about $1,956 by the end of 2025, rising nearly 10% in the past three years, the fastest increase among major U.S. cities. With new apartment construction adding less than 1% to existing supply over the past year, the imbalance between demand and available units is pushing costs higher.

Developers say the core issue is simple: not enough housing is being built. Industry veterans note that large-scale projects are especially scarce, even though those are the developments most needed to ease pressure on rents.

The shift marks a notable change for Chicago, which long stood out for its affordability compared with coastal markets. Rents rose 3.4% last year alone, trailing only San Francisco among major cities, despite Chicago’s comparatively sluggish job growth.

A combination of regulatory hurdles, rising costs and limited financing has discouraged new development. The number of apartment units receiving construction permits has dropped sharply since 2022, and only a handful of large projects are expected to be completed in the near term. Developers warn that the pipeline for 2026 and 2027 remains thin, signaling continued supply constraints.

State and local leaders have acknowledged the problem. Illinois Governor JB Pritzker has pointed to restrictive local rules that increase costs and delay projects, while Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson has launched efforts to streamline approvals. Early steps have reduced initial permitting timelines, though builders say the process is still cumbersome.

Taxes are another concern. Property tax bills in Cook County have risen far faster than inflation over the past three decades, adding uncertainty for developers and property owners alike.

Financing challenges are compounding the issue. Large-scale lending for apartment construction has dried up, with few major loans issued in recent years and traditional lenders pulling back. Investors and capital providers have become increasingly cautious about Chicago projects, making it harder to fund new developments.

Some projects are still moving forward through public incentives or creative financing, and older office buildings are being converted into residential units. But these efforts are not enough to offset the broader slowdown in construction.

For now, Chicago remains cheaper than cities like New York and San Francisco. But without a meaningful increase in housing supply, developers and analysts warn that rents are likely to keep rising, further straining affordability in a market that once prided itself on it.

data.title: Supply shock coming as last oil tankers leaving Hormuz before Iran war to unload in next few days
data.description:
The final oil tankers to traverse the Strait of Hormuz before the outbreak of the Iran war are set to reach refineries by April 20, a pivotal moment that analysts warn could begin physical shortages in Europe and the U.S. within weeks, the Financial Times reported Monday.

Most pre-war deliveries to Asia, which sources ~80% of its crude from the Middle East, stopped around April 1, although at least one cargo of Iraqi oil to Malaysia is expected to land this week, FT reported, while the last pre-war deliveries to the U.S. are set to end this week, those to Africa had landed by April 10, and Denmark received its last jet fuel cargo from Kuwait over the weekend, according to analysts at J.P. Morgan.

Signs of strain in the physical market have emerged in recent days, with prices for cargoes for immediate delivery soaring well above future contracts.

The North Sea's Forties Blend surged to a record $148.87/bbl on Monday, Reuters reported, a highly unusually large premium over the price of Brent crude futures, which are trading at ~$100/bbl, that reflects refiners' desperation to secure supplies.

The supply shock is already causing hardship in countries that are heavily dependent on energy imports from the Middle East, such as the Philippines, Indonesia, and Vietnam.

The last pre-war deliveries coincide with the U.S. imposition of a naval embargo on the Strait of Hormuz aimed at vessels arriving at or leaving Iranian ports, which could choke off the main source of Iranian revenue; the move prompted tanker owners to say they would hesitate before sending new vessels through the chokepoint.

Crude oil futures rose Monday but settled well off earlier highs following reports that the U.S. and Iran still aim to reach an agreement even after talks broke down over the weekend.

The blockade directly endangers Iran's oil exports through the strait, which tracked at ~1.7M bbl/day last month, CBA's Vivek Dhar said in a note, so "the blockade tightens physical oil and refined product markets even further."

The physical scarcity of oil barrels, as reflected in oil benchmarks with physical delivery, has "seen oil exchange hands at prices above $140/bbl in coming weeks," the analyst wrote.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery settled up 2.6% to $99.08/bbl and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery jumped 4.4% to $99.36/bbl, after surging well past $100 in earlier trading.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) gave up early gains and finished lower, with the front-month Nymex May contract ending down 0.8% at $2.627/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Airline blockbuster: United CEO teases interest in merger with American
data.description:
United Airlines (UAL) CEO Scott Kirby has discussed with senior government officials a possible merger with American Airlines Group (AAL), sources tipped Bloomberg. 

A combination of two of the top of the largest U.S. airlines would assuredly face opposition from consumer groups and be scrutinized by regulators since United (UAL) and American (AAL) control more than a third of the U.S. airline market.

Airline industry consolidation is something that Kirby has discussed openly this year, although analysts took his comments to indicate that United (UAL) might try to acquire a smaller regional airline, not combine forces with a legacy player. 

The biggest U.S. airline merger came in 2013, when American Airlines (AAL) and US Airways combined in a deal that created the world’s largest airline by traffic. That deal cemented a highly concentrated industry dominated by American (AAL), Delta (DAL), United (UAL), and Southwest Airlines (LUV). Earlier milestones include American's 2001 purchase of TWA and the 1987 Piedmont Airlines merger into USAir, which was then considered the largest airline merger in U.S. history and an important precursor to the later wave of consolidation. Other blockbuster deals included the 2008–2010 merger of Delta Air Lines (DAL) and Northwest Airlines, which temporarily created the world’s largest airline and significantly expanded Delta's (DAL) global network. In 2010, United Airlines (UAL) combined with Continental Airlines in a multibillion‑dollar all‑stock deal, forming a Chicago‑based giant under the United name with major hubs in Houston and Newark and a broad international footprint.

Shares of American Airlines (AAL) rallied 4.7% in postmarket trading, while United (UAL) gained 1.1%.

data.title: Shipping, tanker stocks rally as Trump launches Strait of Hormuz blockade
data.description:
Stocks in the shipping and tanker industry posted broad gains Monday after the U.S. implemented a naval blockade of Iranian ports following the collapse of weekend peace negotiations, although results finished well off sharp early gains.

The day's gains continued the "disruption" trade in which negative news surrounding the closure of the Strait of Hormuz rallies shipping stocks, while any momentum toward a resolution punishes them, TradeWinds.com noted.

Tanker stocks, which are especially affected by the Iran war, gained an average of 2.8%, according to TradeWinds, with clean product carrier names faring slightly better than the crude tanker group.

Among the day's gainers: Navigator Holdings (NVGS) up 3.7%, Torm (TRMD) up 3.5%, Scorpio Tankers (STNG) up 2.6%, Dorian LPG (LPG) up 2.5%, Nordic American Tankers (NAT) up 2.1%, International Seaways (INSW) up 1.8%, CMB.Tech (CMBT) up 1.6%, Teekay Tankers (TNK) up 1.5%, BW LPG (BWLP) up 1.5%, Ardmore Shipping (ASC) up 1.5%, Frontline (FRO) up 1.4%, Flex LNG (FLNG) up 1.4%, Okeanis Eco Tankers (ECO) up 1.4%, Tsakos Energy Navigation (TEN) up 1.2%, Teekay (TK) up 1%, DHT Holdings (DHT) up 0.6%.

ETFs: (BWET), (BOAT)

data.title: Chevron to swap assets with Venezuela's PDVSA, poised for Orinoco Belt expansion
data.description:
Chevron (CVX) said post-market Monday its subsidiaries with interests in Venezuela agreed to an asset swap with Petroleos de Venezuela, which it said will boost both parties' focus on strategic assets in the country.

Under the deal terms, Chevron (CVX) will receive an additional 13.21% working interest in the Petroindependencia joint venture, raising its total stake to 49% from 35.8% previously, and its Petroplar joint venture, which is 30% owned by Chevron, will receive rights to develop an area located in Venezuela's Orinoco oil belt.

The company also agreed to relinquish the Loran offshore gas field and its stake at a small field in western Venezuela while receiving a new oil area, Ayacucho 8, as ​part of its ​existing Petropiar project, ⁠also in the Orinoco.

In exchange, PDVSA will receive from Chevron (CVX) its 60% and 100% operated interests in the offshore Plataforma Deltana Block 21 and Block 32 gas licenses, respectively, and its 25.2% non-operated interest in the Petroindependiente joint venture located in western Venezuela.

"This agreement expands Chevron's heavy oil position in two key joint ventures in Venezuela and reflects our disciplined development of the country's significant resources," said Javier La Rosa, President of Chevron Base Assets and Emerging Countries.

data.title: Gold and silver turn lower as Middle East peace talks failure rekindles inflation worries
data.description:
Gold and silver futures tilted lower Monday after failed weekend peace talks between the U.S. and Iran reignited worries about the potential for inflation to weigh on global demand for precious metals.

President Trump's call for a U.S. naval blockade at the Strait of Hormuz triggered a move back into energy and away from precious metals, which also continue to move opposite the U.S. dollar, bucking the classic role as a safety play, analysts at RJO Futures said in a note.

Gold pushed off early lows and oil pared some initial gains, however, after Trump said Iran has reached out about peace talks as U.S. forces began the blockade.

"It's a very headline-driven ​market," Blue Line Futures chief market strategist Phillip Streible said in a note. "All eyes are on the price of crude oil because crude oil ​is going to direct inflation, and that is going to direct Federal Reserve policy."

Markets now see a ~29% chance of a U.S. interest rate cut by year-end, according to CME's ​FedWatch Tool, down from ​40% a ⁠month ago, a negative for non-yielding bullion, which benefits from lower borrowing costs.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for April delivery fell 0.4% to $4,742.40/oz, and front-month Comex April silver (XAGUSD:CUR) dropped 1% to $75.523/oz.

data.title: Marvel Biosciences lowers conversion price for proposed debenture offering
data.description:
• Marvel Biosciences (MRVL:CA) on Monday said it has amended the conversion price of its proposed non-brokered private placement of unsecured convertible debentures to $0.17 per share.
  • The company had previously announced plans to raise up to $500,000 through the offering.
  • All other terms of the debentures and the offering remain unchanged, and the transaction is subject to approval by the TSX Venture Exchange.
  • TMXXF closed +1.86% at $37.11.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Greencastle Resources expands stake in Future Fuels via share deal
data.description:
• Greencastle Resources (VGN:CA) will acquire 500,000 shares of Future Fuels, increasing its holdings from 480,000 shares.
  • The deal is valued at $230,000 and will be paid entirely in shares (4.6M Greencastle shares at $0.05 each).
  • No cash will be used in the transaction.

data.title: Stocks to watch after market on Monday: DELL, HPQ, CRDO, GNK
data.description:
Wall Street concluded higher on Monday even as U.S.-Iran talks collapsed, with Washington signaling plans to impose a naval blockade on key Iranian ports.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) jumped up +1.2%, while the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) finished higher by +1%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) gained +0.6%.

Here are the four stocks to watch after market:

Dell Technologies (DELL) fell 3.3% in after-hours trading after Nvidia (NVDA) denied reports that it was in negotiations to acquire a major PC manufacturer. An Nvidia spokesperson told Bloomberg that a SemiAccurate report claiming the company had been in acquisition talks for over a year was “false,” adding that “Nvidia is not engaged in discussions to acquire any PC maker.” The denial reversed earlier gains that saw Dell shares surge approximately 6% during the regular session on the initial speculation.

HP Inc. (HPQ) dropped 3.2% in after-hours trade following Nvidia’s clarification that it is not pursuing a PC maker acquisition. The decline came after shares had jumped around 4% during Monday’s session when the initial SemiAccurate report sparked takeover speculation across the PC sector. Nvidia’s formal denial to Bloomberg effectively unwound the day’s gains for the printer and PC maker.

Credo Technology Group (CRDO) rose 2.69% to $137.91 in after-hours trading after announcing a definitive agreement to acquire DustPhotonics, a developer of Silicon Photonics technology for optical transceivers. The deal includes upfront consideration of $750M in cash and approximately 0.92M shares of Credo common stock, with potential incremental consideration of up to 3.21M additional shares tied to financial milestones. 

Genco Shipping & Trading (GNK) gained 2.8% at Monday’s close amid an ongoing proxy battle with Diana Shipping (DSX). Diana, which owns 14.8% of Genco, sent an open letter to shareholders urging support for its six director nominees at the 2026 annual meeting, criticizing Genco’s board for refusing to engage with two takeover proposals over five months. Diana cited $1.43B in committed funding from six banks and argued the offer provides immediate liquidity with dry bulk asset prices near 15-year highs.
